text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — SURVEILLANCE VISIT
Attending: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
Visit Type: Annual Surveillance — 12-Month Post-Treatment Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Chen is a 53-year-old Taiwanese-American female, never-smoker, presenting for her 12-month post-treatment surveillance visit. She was treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT for HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (left base of tongue, T2N1M0, AJCC 8th ed. stage III), completing chemoradiation approximately 12 months ago. She presents today for routine oncologic surveillance, late toxicity assessment, and review of annual surveillance MRI of the neck.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen completed 70 Gy IMRT with concurrent weekly cisplatin (6 of 7 planned cycles; cumulative dose ~390 mg/m2) roughly 12 months prior to today's visit. Her post-treatment course was notable for grade 3 mucositis requiring a brief hospitalization approximately 2 weeks after completing radiation, as well as prolonged PEG tube dependence through approximately 5 months post-treatment. At her 12-week post-treatment evaluation, PET-CT and MRI of the neck both confirmed a complete metabolic and radiographic response — no evidence of residual disease. Her PEG tube was removed electively at approximately 5 months once oral intake recovered to ~85% of caloric needs. She was last seen in clinic at 6 months post-treatment with no evidence of recurrence.

Since her 6-month visit, she reports continued steady improvement. She denies any new neck masses, throat pain, hemoptysis, otalgia, trismus worsening, or dysphagia progression. She has had no episodes of frank aspiration pneumonia. She notes residual dry mouth, which she manages with pilocarpine and frequent water sips. Taste remains somewhat altered — she estimates about 60% of her pre-treatment flavor perception has returned, with sweet and salty tastes improving more than bitter. She returned to full-time work as a high school principal approximately 4 months ago and reports this has gone well, though she continues to find prolonged speaking or presenting at staff meetings somewhat fatiguing due to xerostomia. She has been doing well at home.

Of note, she presented to endocrinology approximately 2 months ago with symptoms of fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, and constipation. TSH at that time was 12.4 mIU/L with free T4 of 0.6 ng/dL, consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism superimposed on her pre-existing mild hypothyroidism. Her levothyroxine was increased from 50 mcg/day to 100 mcg/day. Today she reports resolution of hypothyroid symptoms. TSH today 2.1 mIU/L — well-controlled on the adjusted dose.

She has been continuing jaw-opening exercises and reports incremental improvement in mouth opening compared to prior visits. She underwent carotid duplex ultrasound at approximately 14 months post-treatment (since she had a new carotid bruit detected on exam at 6 months) — result reviewed today showing mild bilateral carotid intimal-medial thickening without hemodynamically significant stenosis; vascular surgery recommended annual surveillance.

Audiometry was also performed recently: stable bilateral high-frequency threshold shift at 4 kHz, no interval progression from the audiogram done at end of treatment. No subjective hearing complaints.

Dental: On fluoride supplementation. No dental pain, no jaw pain at rest, no exposed bone. Dental oncology following her regularly; no extractions post-radiation have been needed.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Fatigue resolved. No fevers, no night sweats. Weight stable — gained back most of the weight lost during treatment (currently 60.2 kg, essentially at pre-treatment baseline of 62 kg). No unintentional weight loss.
HEENT: Xerostomia grade 1, managed well. Dysgeusia grade 1, slowly improving. Mild bilateral high-frequency hearing loss, stable. Trismus improving. No new neck lumps, throat pain, or voice change.
Cardiovascular: New mild carotid bruit noted on exam; asymptomatic; vascular surgery surveillance underway.
Respiratory: Denies cough, hemoptysis, or dyspnea. No aspiration pneumonia.
Gastrointestinal: PEG removed 7 months ago. Tolerating regular diet with thin liquids. No nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain. Constipation resolved since levothyroxine uptitration.
Genitourinary: No complaints.
Musculoskeletal: Jaw opening improving with exercises.
Neurologic: No new neurologic symptoms.
Endocrine: As above — hypothyroidism now well-controlled on adjusted levothyroxine dose.
Psychosocial: Has participated in a head and neck cancer survivorship support group; reports good psychological adjustment. Back to full professional activities.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 14, temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 60.2 kg. ECOG PS 0.
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Comfortable and in good spirits.
Head: No facial asymmetry. Skin of the bilateral neck with mild hyperpigmentation and mild fibrotic texture changes consistent with prior radiation therapy — no moist desquamation, no ulceration, no signs of skin breakdown or necrosis.
Oropharynx: Oral mucosa moist but mildly xerotic. Tongue freely mobile, no fixed mass or mucosal irregularity. Base of tongue: Post-radiation mucosa without discrete mass or ulceration on visual inspection. Tonsils: Post-radiation changes bilaterally; no visible tumor. No trismus on exam today — mouth opens approximately 40 mm (improved from 35 mm at 6-month visit).
Neck: No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Previously noted left level IIA lymph node not palpable. Soft tissue of the neck mildly fibrotic on the left. Left and right carotid arteries: Mild bruit bilaterally, slightly more prominent on the left, consistent with radiation-induced vascular changes and mild atherosclerosis; no focal neurologic deficits.
Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed by attending at today's visit): Post-radiation mucosal changes of the base of tongue and posterior oropharynx noted — mildly atrophic mucosa, no mucosal irregularity, ulceration, exophytic lesion, or mass. Larynx: Supraglottis, glottis, subglottis appear normal. Vocal cord motion symmetric and complete bilaterally. Vallecula: No pooling of secretions. Hypopharynx: Unremarkable. No suspicious lesion identified.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No S3/S4. Mild bilateral carotid bruit as noted.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG site well-healed without signs of infection or granuloma.
Extremities: No edema.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 12.9 g/dL, Plt 234 x10^9/L — within normal limits.
CMP: Na 139, K 4.1, Cl 102, CO2 25, BUN 14, Cr 0.72 mg/dL (baseline), Ca 9.4. Liver function tests normal.
Magnesium: 1.9 mEq/L (low normal, stable; continuing oral magnesium supplementation at reduced dose).
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (well within normal range on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day — excellent response to dose increase from 50 mcg/day).
Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Annual surveillance MRI neck with contrast (reviewed today): No evidence of locoregional recurrence at the left base of tongue primary site or in the cervical nodal stations. Stable post-treatment mucosal thickening at left base of tongue with mild enhancement; no new discrete mass, ulceration, or suspicious enhancement pattern. The previously noted small left level IIA residual soft tissue (8 mm on 12-week post-treatment MRI, 5 mm on prior check) has further decreased and is essentially resolved. No new cervical adenopathy identified. Mild stable bilateral carotid atherosclerotic change noted — no significant interval progression. No radiation necrosis of mandible or soft tissue. Impression as reported: No evidence of recurrent or new malignancy; changes consistent with treated, complete response.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (left base of tongue, T2N1M0), s/p definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE at 12 months.
Ms. Chen has now achieved and maintained a complete response for one full year. Annual surveillance MRI is negative for any locoregional recurrence. Clinical exam with flexible nasopharyngoscopy is entirely reassuring. This is an outstanding oncologic outcome, consistent with the excellent prognosis we have come to expect from HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal SCC in a never-smoker with a complete response to chemoradiation. I reviewed the results with her in detail and she is understandably very relieved. Continue surveillance per protocol — exam and nasopharyngoscopy every 3-4 months. Chest imaging at 18 months (CT chest without contrast) to complete standard-of-care surveillance interval.

2. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism — now well-controlled.
TSH 2.1 mIU/L on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day. Symptoms fully resolved. Continue current dose; recheck TSH in 4-6 months (sooner if symptoms recur). Endocrinology co-management ongoing.

3. Late radiation toxicity — chronic but manageable.
   - Xerostomia grade 1: Continue pilocarpine 5 mg TID; hydration counseling; Biotene rinses PRN. Significantly improved from the peak of treatment.
   - Dysgeusia grade 1: Continued partial taste recovery expected; zinc supplementation offered but declined by patient at this time; reassurance provided.
   - Trismus: Improving — jaw opening now 40 mm (vs. 35 mm at 6 months). Continue daily Therabite jaw exercises. No intervention required at this time.
   - Dysphagia: Grade 1; tolerating regular diet with thin liquids. No aspiration symptoms. Continue swallowing exercises as per speech therapy. Annual modified barium swallow to be discussed at next visit.
   - Grade 1 ototoxicity: Stable bilateral high-frequency threshold shift at 4 kHz. No functional hearing aids indicated at this time. Annual audiology follow-up.
   - Radiation-related carotid changes: Mild bilateral carotid intimal-medial thickening, no hemodynamically significant stenosis on duplex. Annual carotid duplex per vascular surgery recommendation. Counseled on symptoms of TIA/stroke.
   - No osteoradionecrosis. Dental fluoride use ongoing.

4. Survivorship and quality of life.
Patient has returned to full-time work as a principal with ECOG PS 0. Quality of life appears excellent by patient report. Continuing participation in survivorship support group. Referred to survivorship clinic for ongoing longitudinal monitoring of late effects. HPV vaccination for household contacts (her adolescent children) discussed and encouraged.

5. Next follow-up:
   - Return to head and neck oncology clinic in ~3 months.
   - CT chest without contrast at 18-month interval (in approximately 6 months).
   - Annual MRI neck at 24 months.
   - TSH recheck in 4-6 months.
   - Carotid duplex annually (vascular surgery).
   - Audiology annually.
   - Dental oncology as scheduled.

Total time with patient today: approximately 45 minutes, including flexible nasopharyngoscopy, results review, counseling, and late effects assessment.

— Dictated and signed, Head and Neck Medical Oncology
